tate of Califorllia 
-
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P,O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Lou Conde, Supervisor 
County of San Diego 
Third District 
1600 Pacific Highway 
San Diego 
CA 
92101 
August 23, 1976 
Re: 
Request for Opinion 
No. 76-080 
Dear Supervisor Conde: 
Thank you for your letter of July 31, 1976, request-
ing an opinion with respect to the reporting obligations 
which would attend a contribution of free legal services 
in connection with a prosecution for violation of a local 
campaign disclosure ordinance. 
Because your question 
does not raise a substantial question of interpretation 
under the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be 
issued. 
However, we hope the following informal comments 
will be helpful. 
The donation of free legal services in connection with 
the alleged violation would constitute volunteer personal 
services by the attorney and, as such, would not be a con-
tribution within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
Gov. Code § 82015. 
Accordingly, no reporting obligation 
would ensue as a result of the donated legal services. 
We 
should caution you, however, that the voluntary personal 
services exception extends only to personal services and 
would not be applicable to any costs incurred on your behalf 
by the attorney. 
If, for example, the attorney provides 
supplies or incurs other expenses in connection with the 
litigation, these payments would be in-kind contributions 
and would be reportable. 
If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion re-
quest, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18321. 
If you have any further questions, please contact Kenneth 
Finney I Chief of the Commission I s L~i-vision. 
KF:MB:pvp 
Sincerely, 
( 
~0~ 
Michael Bennett 
Executive Director 


, I 
TYPED FROM HANDWRITTEN LETTER 
uesf NO. __ 
'_~ ___ O_S_O

LOU CONDE 
SUPERVISOR 
THIRD DISTRICT 
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO 
BOARD OF SUPERVISORS 
1600 Pacific Highway 
San Diego: Calif. 92101 
(714) 236-2271 
7/31/76 
Gentlemen: 
Pursuant to Sec. 83114 of ~Title 9 would you 
please advise on the following situation: 
(1) 
If a candidate for a county office 
is charged with a violation of a local 
campaign disclosure ordinance and 'has 
donated legal counsel in the matter is 
he required to report that as an "in-kind" 
contribution? 
Is this considered to be 
a campaign related expense and therefore 
~equired to be reported? 
(2) 
If the answer is yes, what is the 
yardstick to be used to determine appro-
priate amount of contribution? 
(3) 
If the service so performed takes 
place between primary election day and 
the last reporting period for said campaign 
(August 12th) is that contribution to be 
reported by that date? 
I would appreciate as prompt an answer as possible. 
Respectfully, 
/s/ 
Lue Conde 
•

sections.question: If a candidate for a county office is charged with a violation of a local campaign disclosure ordinance and has donated legal counsel in the matter is he required to report that as an "in-kind" contribution? Is this considered to be a campaign related expense and therefore required to be reported? If the answer is yes, what is the yardstick to be used to determine appropriate amount of contribution? If the service so performed takes place between primary election day and the last reporting period for said campaign (August 12th) is that contribution to be reported by that date?
sections.conclusion: The donation of free legal services in connection with the alleged violation would constitute volunteer personal services by the attorney and, as such, would not be a contribution within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Gov. Code § 82015. Accordingly, no reporting obligation would ensue as a result of the donated legal services. However, any costs or expenses incurred by the attorney on behalf of the candidate would constitute in-kind contributions and would be reportable.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the donation of free legal services by an attorney in connection with defending a candidate against campaign disclosure violations constitutes a reportable in-kind contribution under the Political Reform Act, and if so, how it should be valued and when it must be reported.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free legal services provided by an attorney constitute volunteer personal services exempt from reporting under the Political Reform Act. However, any costs or supplies provided by the attorney in connection with the legal services would be reportable in-kind contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal response declining to issue formal opinion (No. 76-080). Document includes both incoming request letter and FPPC response. Clear Q&C structure present with specific questions and answers provided informally.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: If a candidate for a county office is charged with a violation of a local campaign disclosure ordinance and has donated legal counsel in the matter is he required to report that as an "in-kind" contribution? Is this considered to be a campaign related expense and therefore required to be reported? If the answer is yes, what is the yardstick to be used to determine appropriate amount of contribution? If the service so performed takes place between primary election day and the last reporting period for said campaign (August 12th) is that contribution to be reported by that date?

CONCLUSION: The donation of free legal services in connection with the alleged violation would constitute volunteer personal services by the attorney and, as such, would not be a contribution within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Gov. Code § 82015. Accordingly, no reporting obligation would ensue as a result of the donated legal services. However, any costs or expenses incurred by the attorney on behalf of the candidate would constitute in-kind contributions and would be reportable.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declined to issue a formal opinion but provided informal guidance that volunteer legal services are not reportable contributions under the Political Reform Act, though expenses incurred by the attorney would be reportable as in-kind contributions.
